sentences.text: Well, the Gestapo-- that they didn't strip us, that was odd.
Anyone with a fur coat or a glove with fur was taken away, and flashlights were taken away.
They made sure you had no knives or anything of value.
And we had to pay prior to that 50 marks for the transportation, and you were allowed to have 10 marks in your pocket.
And anything what the Germans thought what was not essential, or essential for them, they confiscated.
And it was going on.
There were over 1,000 Jews in the slaughterhouse.
It was going on all night.
Then there was a roll call, and there was one Jew who had no reporting order.
He was with his brother and sister there.
And the Gestapo wanted to force him to go on a transport, and he said no.
And so he had to leave.

Then before daylight came, we were marched out to the railroad tracks in Dusseldorf-Derendorf, and we had to sit there for a couple of hours in the open till the train came with French-- which were French cars.
And each compartment could hold eight people.
And we were loaded up under the supervision of German police and Gestapo, and then there were five policemen in a separate compartment going with us on that train.
And we left around 10 o'clock in the morning.
December 11, 1941.
40 pounds of clothing, and a knapsack with food, and a couple of bottles of water or coffee-- whatever we could-- were allowed to take-- and eating utensils and food.
And-- and for six weeks we had to pack separate, and the clothing went in boxcars.
The entire family.
We were eight in the--
Eight in a compartment together.
Till December 15, 1941.
But on the way to-- between Berlin and Konigsberg, somewhere along the line, something went wrong with the train, and they had to take one of the cars out.
And they pushed peoples in other cars, and we got some people, and they had to lie on the floor because there were no [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] place to sit for them.
Also, we had a problem because we were always sitting day and night.
A lot of them had swelling in the legs because you could not move, and they had edemas in the legs.
No, not all.
You could not leave the train at all.
We could open the window when-- and the train was heated, to our surprise.

And when we arrived somewhere in-- in Lithuania near Schaulen-- Siauliai I think it's in Estonian-- the train stopped, because there was only one train line, because it was a secondary or third train which was not important, because military trains had to go.
The Germans allowed us to get out of the train and to get some water since we did not have anything to drink anymore.
Also there was the problem that a number of toilets were blocked.
And then we were-- after the train left, it went up to Riga.
And when we arrived in Riga in the main railroad station, we had to sit there for two hours.
And then the train was taken out again and it was moved to Skirotava, which was outside of Riga, which had a military ramp which was used by the Latvian army for that purpose.
And we heard the-- the-- the engines were taking off, and within an hour or two everything was ice.
It was bitter cold.
At this time we didn't know anything.
We didn't expect a picnic, but we had not heard of any killing, nothing.
Everything was-- we were cut off from the world.
Nothing-- we even didn't know what was going on in Poland or any country of killing or nothing at that time.
Now, the first thing we saw in the morning when we opened windows that we had guards who were marching along the train.
They were Latvians.
They were in khaki uniform.
They had armbands with a skull and crossbones and a number, and rifles.
And we were told nobody can get out of the train.
Otherwise, you will be shot.

After a while, some Germans arrived-- a Sturmbannfuhrer, or major, and Obersturmfuhrer, which is captain, some Untersturmfuhrer, which are lieutenants, and some NCOs, which was the reception committee.
Then somebody had a bullhorn and announced that we are in a foreign country now.
We are not allowed to have any money or postage stamps.
And therefore two Jews had to go along the train and had to give any money-- we were allowed to take 10 marks along-- put in a hat.
At the same time, we were told anyone who did not follow the orders would be shot.
He said, we have no other choice than follow the orders.
Since he was a member of the German army before, he knew what it was.
So then after they had collected the money and the postage stamps and everything, we got told to get out of the train.
One Jew got out of the train and lit a cigar.
When the Germans saw that, an SS Obersturmfuhrer-- or captain, I think-- walked up to him and beat him up with a cane.
His name was Emil Mayer.
He was from [GERMAN], a city on the Rhine River.
And then we had to line up.
We got told first that our luggage stays behind, except the knapsacks we can carry along.
We have a couple of hours' march ahead.
We had to line up five in a row, and for-- I would say for every 10 or 15 Jews was one armed rifleman who was in khaki uniform with the armband with skull and crossbones and a number.
It was the Latvian SS and police.
Then we were marched to the ghetto.
We were marched to a-- a hole in a wooden fence up to a pile of rubble.
And on this pile of rubble was standing a Jew with a Star of David, the SS Obersturmfuhrer, or captain, and an SS NCO.
And we got addressed.
My name is Max Leiser.
I am the alteste of the Jew of the ghetto.
This is Obersturmfuhrer [NON-ENGLISH], the commandant of the ghetto, and this is his adjutant, [NON-ENGLISH].
And then we heard us a speech.
You cannot go over the fence.
You cannot do-- you only can go on certain streets.
You cannot cross the line of the-- and each sentence ended with the same three words-- will be shot.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After that we were marched to a street, and we got told that we only can go on the right-hand side of the houses.
And we entered the houses and rooms.
We were 20 or 25 in two rooms.
And we got the shock of our life.
The food was frozen on the table.
Clothing was on the floor.
Blood was on the floor.
Blood was on the walls.
It looked as if a hurricane went through.
We looked out of the windows.
Baby carriages were standing outside full with snow, and some which were not full with snow had the blankets and the bottles in it with the milk.
And more people came in.
It was standing room only, because everyone had to be-- the 1,000 people had to be in probably seven or eight houses.
Even some went in the barns and everything, and in the stables, in order to be off the street.
We-- we thought of the pogrom of 1938.
What must have happened over here may be even worse.
But nobody thought of killing then, even though there was blood and clothing on the floor and the walls.
So everyone was flabbergasted and in shock.
We could not see any other people, and we-- later on, we saw some people-- some I know-- that came from Cologne, and we got informed that a transport from Cologne arrived first.
And there were some Jews from Cassel also, and we were the third transport.
And-- and a friend told us, you only can go where they allow you-- otherwise, they shoot you.
And later on we hear a shot ringing out, and so a woman got killed because she had crossed the middle of the street.
And after a while somebody came in and took some people out of the rooms.
More houses were opened up, because there was no other possibility.
So then we walked to find our friends, and it seems, like I said, in the houses and wherever we went standing room only.
So after a while Nussbaum, who was the transport leader and later the alteste of the Dusseldorf group, came in with somebody else.
His name was [GERMAN], which was not an angel.
And they took some of-- said some more houses had been opened up-- and took some people out.
So we were later on probably 20 in two rooms.
So we-- we found some food on the table, which was frozen, and we found a lot of things, and so much clothing and everything.
And we couldn't figure out what happened here.
How did your family settle in, Alfred, into that room?

And so we decided-- there were some mattresses on the floor-- that the older people will sleep on the mattresses tonight and we the younger people will sleep on the floor, because we all had a blanket.
And we made some fire in the oven because there was enough wood.
And we had some sandwiches, and there was water.
And--
There were a number of houses, but we were limited to one street-- to only on the right-hand side of the street from one corner to the other.
On both ends of the street were Latvian guards with rifles.
So if you would go further, they would shoot you.
The name of the street was Virsaisu iela. 'Iela' is street.
But the street was later on named, for the transport of Dusseldorf, Dusseldorfer Strasse.
And the house was number four where I was living in.
Yes.
There were fences all around.
And we saw the fences 24 hours later when we were marched-- we were marched out to the harbor to work.
On the other side from the fence were Jews with two stars, one on the left-hand side of the breast and one in the middle of the back, without any inscription in it-- just yellow stars.
We only saw male.
No female at all.
No.
We could not make any contact.
They were under supervision of German police, and we were under supervision of German army, who marched us out of the ghetto.
But before they-- we were marched out, they took some more people out of those two rooms to give us more place.
There were 25 in those two rooms, and then we were eventually left with 13.
And in one room were two mattresses on the floor, a closet, and a couch.
In the other one was a kitchen table, four chairs, and a couch and the oven.
So we decided that the old people would sleep on the mattresses and on the couch and we the younger people would sleep on the floor.
There was a gate with a couple of guards.
But for every 20 Jews was one German soldier armed with a rifle when we were marched out--
--to the harbor.
Latvian-- Latvian SS or police.
We were marched out of the ghetto shortly after 7 o'clock in the morning, and we arrived by 6 o'clock back in the ghetto.
And we had to go in the houses, and there was a curfew.
You could not go out anymore.
The guard would march you on the street where you were living, and that's when you went into the houses.
There was a family of Nathan Liftman.
It was husband and wife and one daughter, and-- and mother and daughter named Levi.
Nathan was his first name.
Her name I forgot, and the daughter was Alice.
Levi.
The daughter was Emily, and the mother was very well known in Germany as an-- a communist.
And prior to the-- prior to Hitler, she was leading communists in the city of Rheydt, which is next to Munchen-Gladbach, the place where Goebbels came from.
Yes.
There was one oven.
You could cook with wood, and same time it would heat the other room also.
And there was an outhouse for sanitary--
--purpose, and there was running water on the outside.
There was a big stable was full with firewood.
There were no rations whatsoever because we had our-- the knapsacks that we brought in from Germany, we were eating from that--
--and what we found in the house-- what the Jews or the people who left there before left behind.
Nothing.
German only.
First, we had to unload ships for the German army under supervision of soldiers, which were driving us for the first two or three days.

Then, my oldest brother and my younger brother, myself, and a couple of other fellows too, were taken into the food depot, and we had to count cases and bags to be ready-- get them ready to be loaded into boxcars.
And the work had to be done by Russian POWs.
And the Germans told us stay away from the Russians, because they have body lice.
Otherwise, you will be eaten alive by the lice.
When we saw the Russian POWs we got a shock, because they were walking skeletons.
In the same warehouse.
We were.
They were walking skeletons, and the Germans were beating them with rubber truncheons and-- to drive them.
And then we saw the Russians stealing some food.
Well, we saw they were undernourished.
Yes, we could understand, but we couldn't understand why were they were undernourished and what was going on.
Well, we were marched forth and back to the ghetto every day, but we did not get any food from the Germans for the first four days.
Then on the fifth day we got some soup from the army at noontime.
It was 90% water and a potato and a carrot in it, but it was warm, and it was bitter cold.

Then a Russian-- he must have been an officer, the interpreter-- turns his face to the wall while we were eating the soup, and said, I'm talking to the wall that the Germans can't see that I talk to you, because I will be beaten up otherwise.
In a few months you will be looking just like we do, and you will be dying like flies.
The Germans will not feed you.
They will abuse you, and you are expendable just like we are.
We couldn't believe that.
We-- we looked at each other.
But we were a week in the ghetto, and-- we are six days in the ghetto, and we had not get-- gotten a slice of bread yet.
Yes.
We told them what the Russian had said, and they-- and they were shocked.
And my father said, I-- I-- I don't believe it.
Because the Germans are not animals like that.
No.
Not at that time.
Very few.
But we had a little surprise there on the fourth or fifth night in the ghetto.
Some Latvian SS broke in the house and raped one of the girls.
And I will not mention the name, because she's still alive and is in-- lives in New York.
One of the Latvian police.
Yeah.
There were three came in the house, held everyone at bay with a rifle, and then they rape the girl.
Well, everyone said now we know we are in for trouble-- quite a bit.
And the last day we were-- and I think on December 21, everyone got a slice of bread, 100 grams of bread, which is equivalent to three ounces.
And then when we marched back in the ghetto in the evening, we had to line up-- all male had to line up, and everyone over 50 years of age got told he can step out of line.
And we were selected.
We were told we all would go to Salaspils.
We are building a camp for the Jews because the ghetto is too small, that we will have proper living quarters, and things like that.
On the next morning, we were marched out there.
But the selection was done in such a manner that Nussbaum selected more people to go to Salaspils than the SS wanted.
He was very obedient to the SS.
Well, the SS left some people behind.
See, they wanted only 500 Jews that were from the transport-- Hanover, Dusseldorf, and Cassel.
Each transport had 1,000 Jews when they came in, but they wanted only 500 males of the-- of the 3,000.
And they didn't want any children, and they didn't want any older people.
We knew that Leiser was the alteste of the Jews, but that's all we knew, because the minute we walked in the ghetto we were in the houses.
There was a curfew.
You could not go out.
You could not talk to anyone.
And it was too cold, and snow and everything.
You were glad to be in warmth, because all day long we were out in the cold working for the army.
Not during that time.
No.
In the ghetto.
They were to stay in the ghetto.
They stay in the ghetto.
And my brother-in-law had an accident.
He fell in-- in the harbor.
He stayed behind.
He was not selected.
My oldest and my youngest brother.
Kurt and Herbert.
Yes.
We had to march to Salaspils, which was 17 kilometers, which is roughly 11 miles, through ice and snow, under supervision of Latvian SS and police.
For each 10 Jews you had one rifleman.
The march took roughly four or five hours because it was very difficult to march.
When we arrived in Salaspils, we saw two barracks, one barrack partially built, and a tent.
And we had to stay outside, and a lot of yelling was going on.
Then one fellow Jew came out and introduced himself as [GERMAN] Wertheim, that he is the leader of barrack number two, where we Jews would go in.
No.
He was the barrack leader of-- then there was Jewish police.
One fellow introduced himself as [GERMAN].
He was the elder of the camp.
And then we were sorted out in groups and got told we are going to sleep on bunks.
Each bunk will hold three persons.
So we brothers said we stay together.
Then we were-- when we were marched in the barrack, we got the surprise of our lives.
There were not-- were not bunks.
There were shelves.
There were three shelves on each side of the barrack and five shelves in the middle.
And the shelf was, oh, I would say six feet wide and seven feet long, and on each shelf three persons had to sleep.
But the only surprise we got that on the shelves were mattresses.
When we looked at the labels on the mattresses, they had the name of Jews, and the addresses were from Berlin.
But there were no Jews from Berlin in the ghetto at that time.
Then later on we met Jews from Hamburg, Stuttgart, and Nuremberg, and we found out that they were in Jungfernhof, which was halfway between the ghetto and Salaspils.
They told us that Salaspils was originally a Russian airport, a farm which the Russians had made an airport from.
And in one of the hangars, which was originally a big barn, all males were put in, and in the stables the women and children.
